It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! LEFT brought the frame back to [[24, 25]], but UP and DOWN didn't change anything. Only RIGHT moves the frame (to [[21, 39]]). So:
- LEFT: moves frame left (back to original)
- RIGHT: moves frame right (and up)
- UP: no effect
- DOWN: no effect
Wait, actually the frame only moved with RIGHT. Let me re-examine:
- After LEFT: frame at [[24, 25]] (back to original position)
- After UP: frame at [[24, 25]] (no change)
- After DOWN: frame at [[24, 25]] (no change)
- After RIGHT: frame at [[21, 39]] (moved right and up)
So it seems like only RIGHT moves the frame. But LEFT also moved it back. Let me check if LEFT works from the moved position.
Actually wait, I'm running out of actions (45 of 55). Let me think more carefully about what the goal could be.
Looking at the image again, the frame is now at a diagonal position. The id=12 object inside appears as a diagonal white shape. The id=13 white block is below and to the left.
What if the goal is to position the frame so that id=12 overlaps with id=13? Or what if the goal is to move the frame to a specific location?
Let me try LEFT from the current position to go back, then try RIGHT again and see if I can move the frame in smaller increments.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the frame movement is the key mechanic. What if I need to move the frame to overlap with the left panel (the white/purple reference)?
Or what if the goal is to position the frame so that when I press SPACE, something special happens?
Let me try moving the frame to different positions and see if SPACE does something different.
Actually, I'm running low on actions. Let me be more strategic. Let me first go back to the original position with LEFT, then think about what to try.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Frame is at [[21, 39]] after RIGHT. Let me go back with LEFT and test if UP/DOWN work from original position
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [11, 12, 13]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} top_left={b[0]}")

# Now try multiple RIGHT to see if frame keeps moving
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter RIGHT, RIGHT:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [11, 12, 13]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} top_left={b[0]}")

# Try LEFT to go back
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT'])
seg3 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter LEFT, LEFT:")
for n in seg3['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [11, 12, 13]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} top_left={b[0]}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

UP/DOWN had no effect from original position.", "goal_model": "Maybe position frame to overlap id=13 or left panel?", "recent_findings": ["LEFT reverses RIGHT movement", "UP/DOWN had no effect"], "open_questions": ["Can frame reach id=13 position? What happens when frame overlaps other objects?"], "current_plan": "Test if frame can be moved to overlap id=13", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["UP from original: nothing", "DOWN from original: nothing"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
